description: A writer puts words on paper, a screen, or a tactile surface so that someone can read them later. 'thekiro' creates written language, whether by hand or tool. 'sirami' (record) preserves an account: a written poem is writing even when it records no event, and a sound recording is a record without being writing.
articulatory_notes: Begin 'the' by resting the tongue tip against the upper teeth and letting unvoiced breath pass before middle-height front 'e'. Raise the rear of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release into stressed high-front 'i'. Finish 'ro' by briefly tapping or trilling against the alveolar ridge, then move into rounded middle-height back 'o'.
sound_symbolism: The word shares opening 'the' with 'theo' (read). Its middle closes sharply for 'k' before the tongue taps into final 'ro'; the contact and release give the longer member of the pair a hand-worked feel.
usage_notes: The text or expression being written precedes the verb. Inchoative 'pa' marks the beginning of the activity; noun-position 'thekiro' is writing or a body of written work. The chosen script or medium can be stated separately when it matters.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Writing can give a speaker time to revise before their words enter a relationship, and it gives the reader time to return before answering.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Hand-copied records and stories carried knowledge beyond one memory and one lifetime. The work depended on surfaces, tools, and people willing to make the marks again.

semantic_domains.communication: creating language in a form that can be read later
semantic_domains.creation: making a written text by hand or tool
